text: The intraoperative view shows the abdomen of a patient with rectal cancer undergoing laparoscopic anterior resection. The inferior mesenteric vein has been ligated, and the mesentery is prepared. An arrow marks the planned transection point under white light.

The screen displays a split view with three smaller boxes on the left and a larger box. The top left box shows a white light image, the middle left box presents a black and white image displaying the near-infrared signal, and the bottom left box combines white light and near-infrared images, showing ICG as fluorescent green.

The intravenous injection of ICG results in a fluorescent signal illuminating the surrounding tissue, except at the planned transection point, which shows no inflow. The colon lights up more proximally, indicating the need to adjust the transection point to a better perfused section of the bowel.

The video shows the migration of green fluorescent coloration distally over a six-minute period, making it difficult to determine the optimal transection point as the transition zone shifts. The late dye creep complicates the interpretation of the fluorescence angiogram.

In the laparoscopic view, ICG is used to demarcate the transition point in extracorporeal anastomosis after a right hemicolectomy. The proximal small bowel is visible in the top right of the video, while the distal large bowel is in the top left. The devascularized ileocolic territory does not uptake ICG, and the dye is seen creeping slowly from the initial margin, complicating the interpretation of the near-field angiogram.

The surgical image displays three distinct zones: the purple zone, representing the devascularized ileocolic territory near the cecum, which does not fluoresce; the red zone, indicating the area near the blood supplies for the ascending colon and ileum, marked by a plus sign; and the green zone, which is the transition zone located centrally in the image.
